chunks.text: Queues and Priority Queues Chapter 13 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The ADT Queue • Like a line of people – First person in line is first person served – New elements of queue enter at its back – Items leave the queue from its front • Called FIFO behavior – First In First Out © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The ADT Queue FIGURE 13-2 Some queue operations © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The ADT Queue FIGURE 13-2 Some queue operations © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The ADT Priority Queue • Organize data by priorities – Example: weekly “to do” list • Priority value – We will say high value  high priority • Operations – Test for empty – Add to queue in sorted position – Remove/get entry with highest priority © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The ADT Priority Queue © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The ADT Priority Queue FIGURE 13-4 UML diagram for the class PriorityQueue © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved FIGURE 13-5 UML diagram for the class Assignment Using a priority queue, we can • organize these assignments in due date order • task with the earliest due date will have the highest priority • define a class Assignment of tasks that includes a data field date representing a task’s due date Tracking Your Assignments Pseudocode to organize assignments, responsibilities © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Application: Simulation • Simulation, a major application area for computers, is models the behavior of both natural and human-made systems • Mathematicians learned to model events (e.g., the arrival of people) by using techniques from probability theory. • This statistical information is incorporated into the mathematical model of the system and is used to generate events in a way that reflects the real world. • The simulation uses these events and is thus called an event driven simulation. • The use of a data file with predetermined event information is common in simulations. • Problem to solve – Approximate average time bank customer must wait for service from a teller – Decrease in customer wait time with each new teller added © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Application: Simulation Sample arrival and transaction times © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Application: Simulation FIGURE 13-6 A bank line at time (a) 0; (b) 20; (c) 22; (d) 26 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Application: Simulation Pseudocode for an event loop © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Application: Simulation • Time-driven simulation – Simulates the ticking of a clock – Increment time counter • Event-driven simulation considers – Only the times of certain events, – In this case, arrivals and departures • Event list contains – All future arrival and departure events © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Application: Simulation FIGURE 13-7 A typical instance of (a) an arrival event; (b) a departure event © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Application: Simulation • Two tasks required to process each event – Update the bank line: Add or remove customers. – Update the event

the times of certain events, – In this case, arrivals and departures • Event list contains – All future arrival and departure events © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Application: Simulation FIGURE 13-7 A typical instance of (a) an arrival event; (b) a departure event © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Application: Simulation • Two tasks required to process each event – Update the bank line: Add or remove customers. – Update the event queue: Add or remove events. • New customer – Always enters bank line – Served while at the front of the line © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Application: Simulation FIGURE 13-8 A trace of the bank simulation algorithm for the data (20, 6), (22, 4), (23, 2), (30, 3) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Position-Oriented and Value-Oriented ADTs • Position-oriented ATDs – Stack, list, queue • Value-oriented ADTs – Sorted list - When a new item is added to a sorted list, the sorted list determines the item’s placement according to its value. • Unlike a list, a sorted list cannot be told where to place its items. – Priority queue - orders its entries according to their priorities © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Position-Oriented and Value-Oriented ADTs © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Stack Queue isEmpty - isEmpty push - insert a new item on top enqueue - insert a new item at end pop - removes the most recent item at top of stack dequeue removes the first item, which is at the front of the queue peek - retrieves the most recent item, which is at the top of the stack peekFront - retrieves the first item at the front of the queue Position-Oriented and Value-Oriented ADTs • ADT list operations generalize stack and queue operations – getLength – insert – remove – getEntry © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved End Chapter 13 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Appendix Chapter 13 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Applications Recognizing a Palindrome FIGURE 13-3 The results of inserting the characters a, b, c, b, d into both a queue and a stack • Remove characters from front of queue, top of stack • Compare each pair removed • If all pairs match, string is a palindrome © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Position-Oriented and Value-Oriented ADTs • Comparison of stack and queue operations – isEmpty - see if any items exist in the ADT – push and enqueue - insert a new item into one end (the top and back, respectively) of the ADT – pop and dequeue - removes the most recent item, which is at the top of the stack, and dequeue removes the first item, which is at the front of the queue – peek and peekFront - peek operation retrieves the most recent item, which is at the top of the stack, and peekFront retrieves the first item at the front of the queue © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
